mcqs.question: When a derived class overrides a virtual function from the base class, what happens to the virtual attribute?

options.text: It must be explicitly specified again
It is lost and becomes non-virtual

mcqs.explanation: The virtual attribute is inherited automatically. Once a function is declared virtual in a base class, all overriding functions in derived classes remain virtual without needing to specify the virtual keyword.

mcqs.question: What is required for achieving runtime polymorphism in C++?
mcqs.explanation: Runtime polymorphism is achieved through virtual functions which enable dynamic binding - the function call is resolved at runtime based on the actual object type.

mcqs.question: What does a pure virtual function declaration look like?
mcqs.explanation: A pure virtual function is declared using the '= 0' syntax, which makes the function abstract with no implementation.

mcqs.question: What is an abstract class?

options.text: A class with all pure virtual functions
A class with at least one pure virtual function
A class with no virtual functions
A class with only private members

mcqs.correctAnswer: A class with at least one pure virtual function
mcqs.explanation: A class containing at least one pure virtual function is called an abstract class and cannot be instantiated.

mcqs.question: Why should base class destructors be virtual?

options.text: To ensure proper cleanup of derived class objects

mcqs.correctAnswer: To ensure proper cleanup of derived class objects
mcqs.explanation: Virtual destructors ensure that when deleting through a base pointer, the derived class destructor is also called, preventing memory leaks.

mcqs.question: What is the vptr?
mcqs.explanation: vptr is a hidden pointer added by the compiler to each object of a class with virtual functions, pointing to the class's vtable.

mcqs.question: What is slicing in C++?

options.text: Losing derived class portion when assigned to base by value

mcqs.correctAnswer: Losing derived class portion when assigned to base by value
mcqs.explanation: Slicing occurs when a derived object is assigned to a base object by value, the derived part is 'sliced off' and polymorphism is lost.

mcqs.question: Which keyword helps catch overriding errors at compile-time?
mcqs.explanation: The override keyword explicitly indicates that a function should override a virtual function, and the compiler will error if no matching base function exists.

mcqs.question: Can constructors be virtual in C++?
mcqs.explanation: Constructors cannot be virtual because they are called to create objects, and the object's type is known at compile time.

mcqs.question: What is the purpose of the 'final' keyword with virtual functions?
mcqs.explanation: When a virtual function is marked final, derived classes cannot override that function.

mcqs.question: What is the size overhead per object when a class has virtual functions?

options.text: Depends on number of virtual functions

mcqs.explanation: Each object with virtual functions has a vptr added, which is typically 4 bytes (32-bit) or 8 bytes (64-bit systems).

mcqs.question: What is function hiding in C++?

options.text: When a function is not used
When derived class function hides base class functions with same name but different signature
When a virtual function is not called
When a private function is accessed

mcqs.correctAnswer: When derived class function hides base class functions with same name but different signature
mcqs.explanation: Function hiding occurs when a derived class declares a function with the same name as a base class function but with a different signature, hiding all base class versions.

mcqs.question: What is early binding?
mcqs.explanation: Early binding (static binding) occurs when the function call is resolved at compile time, as with non-virtual functions.

mcqs.question: What is late binding (dynamic binding)?

options.text: Binding at runtime when object type is known

mcqs.correctAnswer: Binding at runtime when object type is known
mcqs.explanation: Late binding occurs at runtime, where the function to be called is determined by the actual object type through the vptr/vtable mechanism.

mcqs.question: What happens if a derived class does not override a pure virtual function?
mcqs.explanation: If a derived class doesn't override a pure virtual function, it also becomes abstract and cannot be instantiated.

mcqs.question: Which access specifier can virtual functions have in base class?
mcqs.explanation: Virtual functions can have any access specifier in the base class, though typically they are public for polymorphic behavior.

mcqs.question: What is the order of destructor calls in inheritance?
mcqs.explanation: Destructors are called in reverse order of construction - derived class destructor first, then base class destructors.

mcqs.question: Can static functions be virtual in C++?
mcqs.explanation: Static functions cannot be virtual because they are not bound to any object and there's no 'this' pointer to use for dynamic dispatch.

mcqs.question: What is a virtual inheritance?

options.text: Inheritance to avoid multiple copies of base class

mcqs.correctAnswer: Inheritance to avoid multiple copies of base class
mcqs.explanation: Virtual inheritance is used in multiple inheritance to ensure only one copy of the base class exists in the inheritance hierarchy.

mcqs.question: What is the main advantage of using virtual functions?
mcqs.back: Runtime polymorphism with the same interface for different object types
mcqs.explanation: Virtual functions allow the same function call to behave differently based on the actual object type, enabling polymorphic behavior at runtime.
